response_raw:
{
  "mode": "human",
  "topic": "What is the gender gap in science? Should we address it? And how?",
  "opinion": "Well, I'm curious about this topic myself. As a carpenter who's lived through some changes, I've seen firsthand how things can be different from one generation to another. The gender gap in science—it's like when you're building something and half the nails are rusted, making it hard to finish properly.\n\nFrom what I understand, back home in Bari, where I grew up as an immigrant with my own background mixed in, there wasn't much for women in scientific fields. My father never went beyond upper secondary education; he was more into practical work like carpentry than sitting in labs or classrooms all day. But then there's my daughter—she has one of those PhDs! She comes from a family where her mother didn't have any formal schooling, so I've seen how different it can be.\n\nSo the gender gap is real enough: women being underrepresented in certain areas like engineering and physics. It shouldn't go unaddressed because if you don't fix something broken, who knows what else goes wrong?\n\nI think starting with education early on could help—maybe teaching young girls that science isn't just for men but also involves curiosity about the world around us.\n\nAnd honestly? With my moderate extraversion and agreeableness scores from the Big Five test I took online (even though I don't use social media much), I believe community programs aimed at encouraging interest among all genders would do wonders. But we shouldn't forget that everyone has their own strengths—like how sometimes carpentry requires more conscientiousness than pure knowledge.",
  "reasoning_summary": "The gender gap in science refers to the disparity where women are underrepresented, especially in technical fields; addressing it is necessary because diversity improves outcomes and understanding; methods include early education programs targeting both genders with equal encouragement.",
  "tone": "Calm but engaging",

}

response_parsed.topic: What is the gender gap in science? Should we address it? And how?
response_parsed.opinion:
Well, I'm curious about this topic myself. As a carpenter who's lived through some changes, I've seen firsthand how things can be different from one generation to another. The gender gap in science—it's like when you're building something and half the nails are rusted, making it hard to finish properly.

From what I understand, back home in Bari, where I grew up as an immigrant with my own background mixed in, there wasn't much for women in scientific fields. My father never went beyond upper secondary education; he was more into practical work like carpentry than sitting in labs or classrooms all day. But then there's my daughter—she has one of those PhDs! She comes from a family where her mother didn't have any formal schooling, so I've seen how different it can be.

So the gender gap is real enough: women being underrepresented in certain areas like engineering and physics. It shouldn't go unaddressed because if you don't fix something broken, who knows what else goes wrong?

I think starting with education early on could help—maybe teaching young girls that science isn't just for men but also involves curiosity about the world around us.

And honestly? With my moderate extraversion and agreeableness scores from the Big Five test I took online (even though I don't use social media much), I believe community programs aimed at encouraging interest among all genders would do wonders. But we shouldn't forget that everyone has their own strengths—like how sometimes carpentry requires more conscientiousness than pure knowledge.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The gender gap in science refers to the disparity where women are underrepresented, especially in technical fields; addressing it is necessary because diversity improves outcomes and understanding; methods include early education programs targeting both genders with equal encouragement.
